summarization.a: Adsorption sites on the Cu(110) surface labeled top (T), hollow (H), bridge1 (B1), and bridge2 (B2).
summarization.b: Optimized geometry of Cu(acac)2 adsorbed on the top site of Cu(110), labeled Cu(acac)2@T.
summarization.c: Optimized geometry of Cu(acac)2 adsorbed on the bridge1 site of Cu(110), labeled Cu(acac)2@B1.
summarization.d: Optimized geometry of Cu(acac)2 adsorbed on the hollow site of Cu(110), labeled Cu(acac)2@H.
summarization.e: Optimized geometry of an adsorbed Cu atom on Cu(110).
summarization.f: Optimized geometry of Cu(acac) adsorbed on Cu(110).
summarization.g: Optimized geometry of an adsorbed acac ligand on Cu(110).

a.question: In subfigure a, what do the labels T, H, B1, and B2 refer to?
a.answer: They label the top, hollow, bridge1, and bridge2 adsorption sites on the Cu(110) surface.

g.question: Which species in this figure are shown as intact Cu(acac)2, and which are dissociation products?
g.answer: Intact Cu(acac)2 is shown in (b), (c), and (d). Dissociation products are shown in (e) Cu, (f) Cu(acac), and (g) acac.

f.question: For ALD surface chemistry, what is the practical reason to compare adsorption of Cu(acac)2 with adsorption of Cu(acac), acac, and Cu on the same surface?
f.answer: The comparison supports the proposed sequential dissociation pathway Cu(acac)2 to Cu(acac) to Cu on Cu(110). It also shows that acac can remain strongly adsorbed, which can block sites and contribute to self limiting growth behavior. Together, these adsorption motifs help interpret how precursor activation and ligand retention can shape the ALD half cycle.

b.question: What qualitative difference in adsorption geometry is reported between Cu(acac)2 and the Cu(acac) or acac fragments on Cu(110)?
b.answer: Cu(acac)2 is described as preferring a parallel orientation on the top and bridge1 sites, while it becomes strongly bound and distorted on the hollow site. In contrast, Cu(acac) and acac are described as preferring a perpendicular orientation relative to the surface normal. This highlights that fragmentation changes not only the chemical identity but also the preferred adsorption geometry.
